meta.description: LLM Agent Dev Tips — daily tips for SvelteKit + TypeScript developers
categories: Adversarial Prompting as a Design Tool
Updating Devs in a Smart Way

tips.title: Context Windows as Architecture
tips.subtitle: Feed Agents the Right Slice
tips.summary: An LLM agent is only as good as the context you hand it — structuring your SvelteKit codebase so that any feature's full picture fits in a single, coherent context window is the highest-leverage architectural decision you can make when working with coding agents.
tips.subject: DevTip #001 — Context Windows as Architecture

tips.title: Agent-First API Design
tips.subtitle: Types Are the Spec
tips.summary: A well-typed, self-documenting TypeScript API surface lets an LLM agent reason about, extend, and debug your routes without ever needing you to explain business logic — your code is the documentation.
key_concepts: Consistent REST to service function naming (1:1 mapping)
Zod schemas as single source of truth
JSDoc as machine-readable business logic spec
Auto-generate OpenAPI spec from Zod at build time
tips.subject: DevTip #002 — Agent-First API Design

tips.title: Prompt Chaining for UI Generation
tips.subtitle: Consistent Components Every Time
tips.summary: One-shot UI generation from LLM agents produces inconsistent, design-system-ignorant components — breaking the chain into discrete, primed steps produces output you can ship without a rewrite.
key_concepts: 4-step UI generation chain: prime, contract, generate, harden
Design tokens file as agent context primer
tips.subject: DevTip #003 — Prompt Chaining for UI Generation

tips.title: Tools aren’t “APIs”; they’re contracts: design tool schemas like you design libraries
tips.subtitle: If a tool can’t fail predictably, your agent can’t recover.
tips.summary: Most agent failures aren’t “model problems” — they’re interface problems. If your tool call surface is underspecified (ambiguous inputs, untyped outputs, inconsistent errors), the agent can’t plan, can’t validate, and can’t recover.
tips.subject: DevTip #004 — Tools aren’t “APIs”; they’re contracts: design tool schemas like you design libraries

tips.title: Migrations as a Safety Harness for Agents
tips.subtitle: Make schema changes legible, reversible, and reviewable
tips.summary: A migration workflow that separates DDL, idempotent backfills, and verification queries makes agent-driven schema changes safe to ship.
tips.subject: DevTip #005 — Migrations as a Safety Harness for Agents

tips.title: Tokens as a Design Budget
tips.subtitle: Turn prompt length into an explicit cost model for features
tips.summary: Treat tokens like a constrained palette: define a per-layer token budget (system, app context, retrieval, user, output), enforce it deterministically, and fail closed for high-stakes flows so prompt bloat becomes a reviewable, debuggable event.
key_concepts: Fail-closed on hard truncation for high-stakes tasks
tips.subject: DevTip #006 — Tokens as a Design Budget

tips.title: Zero Downtime agent deployments on Railway
tips.subtitle: Ship without interrupting running agent sessions
tips.summary: Use explicit readiness plus SIGTERM draining so Railway can shift traffic to new instances without breaking in flight agent sessions.
tips.subject: DevTip #007 -- Zero Downtime agent deployments on Railway

tips.title: Contract Tests for Agent Tool Interfaces
tips.subtitle: Every tool your agent calls needs a test that runs in CI
tips.summary: Agent-written code ships fast -- so fast that the seams between your tools and the agent become the highest-risk surface. Contract tests lock the tool interface down so CI breaks before the agent ever runs.
tips.subject: DevTip #008 -- Contract Tests for Agent Tool Interfaces

tips.title: RAG Quality Gates for Production Agents
tips.subtitle: Measure retrieval before the model sees it
tips.summary: Add cheap retrieval signals and CI tests so RAG stays reliable
tips.subject: DevTip #009 -- RAG Quality Gates for Production Agents
